Lab 5: Sensor Dashboard GUI

This lab completes a two-lab design. In Lab 4 you built SensorDataAnalyzer — the analysis engine. In Lab 5 you are building the interface that drives it. The separation between the two was deliberate. By the end of this lab you should be able to articulate exactly why, and what would be lost if they were combined.

Reminder: The Three-Phase Structure

Every lab in this course follows the same pattern established in Lab 1:

PRE-LAB → LAB SESSION → POST-LAB (before) (in person) (after)

The documentation principle from Lab 1 applies here with one important adjustment: the key documentation in this lab is app screenshots, not saved PNG plots. Take every screenshot before closing the app — unlike a plot script, a running app cannot be regenerated from code alone without re-running the experiment.

Goals

In this lab you will complete the two-lab design by building a graphical interface that drives your SensorDataAnalyzer class. By the end you will have:

  • Built a working sensor dashboard GUI in MATLAB AppDesigner
  • Connected button callbacks to existing analysis methods without rewriting the analysis logic
  • Demonstrated your app collecting, smoothing, integrating, and resetting sensor data
  • Articulated why separating interface code from analysis code was the deliberate choice for this design

Concepts applied: Event-driven programming, AppDesigner callbacks, object-oriented design, separation of concerns

PRE-LAB

Complete everything in this section before your lab session. Parts 1 and 2 are assigned after Lab 4 submission — complete them in the gap between labs.

Pre-Lab Part 1: AI Exploration (complete AFTER Lab 4 is submitted, BEFORE Lab 5 session)

This lab is built around a load-bearing concept: separation of GUI code from analysis logic (separation of concerns). You have already lived both sides of this design: you built the analysis class in Lab 4 and tested every method directly from the MATLAB command line before any GUI existed. In Lab 5 you will build the interface that calls those same methods. The AI exploration below prepares you to understand why the design was structured this way — and what it makes possible.

Step 1: Open an AI assistant of your choice.

Step 2: Use the following as your opening prompt:

"I am a first-year engineering student who just built a data analysis class called SensorDataAnalyzer in MATLAB. It has methods for logging sensor data, selecting a signal, integrating it, and plotting it. I tested all these methods by calling them directly from the MATLAB command line before any GUI existed. Now in my next lab, I am going to build a GUI that calls these same methods through button callbacks. My instructor designed it this way deliberately — all the analysis stays in the class, none of it is written inside the GUI callbacks. What I don't fully understand is: why does it matter that the analysis is in the class rather than written directly inside the callback functions? What would actually go wrong in practice if I mixed them together?"

Step 3: Follow up with at least two of these questions to push the explanation further:

  • "If someone asked me to replace the MATLAB GUI with a web interface for the same analysis, how would the separation make that easier or harder?"
  • "Can you give me an example from industry where this separation between interface and logic is standard?"
  • "I tested my integrate() method by calling it directly from the command line before I had any GUI. What did that experience actually demonstrate about the separation?"

Step 4: Verify that the AI's explanation connects to your own Lab 4 experience. Specifically: does it acknowledge that testing methods from the command line — before the GUI existed — was only possible because the analysis lived in the class, not inside callbacks? If the AI gives a generic software-design answer without connecting to this, push back and ask it to tie the explanation to that specific testing scenario.

Step 5: Write a 3–5 sentence summary of what you learned in your own words. Keep it — it becomes Field 1 of your post-lab submission.

Why this matters now: In Lab Step 3, you will add callbacks one at a time and test each one before moving to the next. The reason this is possible — that each callback is short and does not contain analysis logic — is a direct consequence of the separation you explored in this pre-lab. Students who skip this step add callbacks and test them, but do not understand what they are doing or why.

Pre-Lab Part 2: Self-Test (complete AFTER Lab 4 is submitted, BEFORE Lab 5 session)

This step confirms that you have internalized the concept — not just read about it.

Step 1: Ask the AI the following prompt:

"Give me a scenario where a developer mixed GUI code and analysis code, and something went wrong. What was the problem?"

Step 2: Read the scenario. Before asking the AI anything further, identify in writing which of the following types of problem the scenario illustrates:
- Testability — the analysis could not be tested without launching the full GUI
- Reusability — the analysis could not be called from a different context (a script, a different interface)
- Fragility — a change to the interface broke working analysis code, or vice versa

More than one may apply. Record your classification and your reasoning.

Step 3: Note the full scenario (in your own words — not a paste) and your classification in your notes document. This record becomes Field 2 of your post-lab submission.

Pre-Lab Checklist

  • [ ] Lab 4 submitted before beginning Parts 1 and 2
  • [ ] AI Exploration (Part 1) completed; 3–5 sentence summary written and saved
  • [ ] Can answer in your own words: what goes wrong in practice when GUI and analysis code are mixed?
  • [ ] Self-Test (Part 2) completed; scenario, classification, and reasoning recorded in notes
  • [ ] SensorDataAnalyzer.m from Lab 4 accessible and ready to copy into your Lab 5 folder

LAB SESSION

Complete this section during your in-person lab period. TAs are available to help.

Bring to the lab session:
- Laptop with MATLAB Online accessible
- Smartphone with MATLAB Mobile installed and signed in
- Your SensorDataAnalyzer.m file from Lab 4 (from your MiniProject4 folder on MATLAB Drive)
- Notes document open and ready

Documentation Reminder

This lab's primary deliverables are screenshots of the running app — not saved PNG files. Every time you reach a key state of the app:

  1. Take a screenshot immediately — do not close the app first
  2. Note what the screenshot shows in your notes document
  3. Save the screenshot to a location you can access after the session

Take your four required screenshots before closing the app. Unlike a MATLAB script, a running app cannot be re-executed after the session to regenerate missing screenshots without repeating the physical experiment.

Background: AppDesigner and Event-Driven Programming

Concept box — read before starting Step 1

AppDesigner is MATLAB's tool for building GUIs without writing layout code by hand. You drag and drop components onto a canvas, arrange them visually, and then write the callback functions that respond to user actions.

A callback is a function that runs in response to a user action — a button press, a dropdown change — not in sequence from top to bottom like a script, but triggered by events. This is called event-driven programming: the order in which code executes is determined by what the user does, not by the structure of the file.

The analysis logic inside SensorDataAnalyzer runs sequentially within its methods — only the triggering of those methods is event-driven. A callback that calls app.Analyzer.integrate() is brief: it triggers a method, re-plots the result, and exits. All the integration math lives in the class, untouched by the GUI layer.

This is exactly why the separation matters. The analysis methods work correctly regardless of whether they are called from a GUI callback, the command line, or a test script. You already verified this in Lab 4 — you tested integrate() from the command line before any GUI existed. That was only possible because the logic was in the class.

Lab Step 1: Layout GUI Components (Design View)

Open AppDesigner: in MATLAB Online, navigate to the Apps tab and click Design App, or type appdesigner in the command window.

A blank canvas opens in Design View. Drag and drop the following components from the Component Library on the left:

Edit Fields (Numeric) — three total:
- Delay (seconds)
- Duration (seconds)
- Window Size

Buttons — four total:
- Collect Data
- Smooth Signal
- Integrate Target Signal
- Reset Target Signal

Drop Down:
- Set Target Signal — set the dropdown items to: X-Accel, Y-Accel, Z-Accel, R-Accel

UIAxes:
- One main plot area — this should be the largest element on the canvas

Image:
- A placeholder for your logo (you will assign the image file in Step 4)

Layout requirements:
- All controls must be visible and accessible without scrolling
- Group related controls together (e.g., Delay, Duration, and Collect Data together; Window Size and Smooth Signal together)
- The axes should occupy the majority of the canvas area

Save the app file as SensorDashboard.mlapp in a new folder:

MATLAB Drive └── ENG6 └── MiniProject5 ├── SensorDashboard.mlapp └── SensorDataAnalyzer.m ← copy from MiniProject4

Important: SensorDataAnalyzer.m must be in the same folder as SensorDashboard.mlapp. Copy it from your Lab 4 folder before running the app. AppDesigner cannot find the class if the file is elsewhere.

Lab Step 2: Add Startup Function and App Property (Code View)

Switch to Code View by clicking the Code View button at the top of AppDesigner.

Add a public property called Analyzer. In the Code View editor, locate the properties block or add one:

matlab properties (Access = public) Analyzer % SensorDataAnalyzer instance end

Add a startup function. In the Component Browser or Callbacks menu, add a startupFcn callback:

MathWorks Docs: startupFcn — App Designer startup function; see "Manage Code in App Designer Code View" for how and when to use it

matlab function startupFcn(app) app.Analyzer = SensorDataAnalyzer(); end

This creates a SensorDataAnalyzer instance when the app launches and stores it as app.Analyzer. Every callback you write will use app.Analyzer to call analysis methods.

Run the app using the green play button. If it launches without errors, the constructor is working correctly and MATLAB can find SensorDataAnalyzer.m. If you see a "class not found" error, confirm both files are in the same folder and that folder is the MATLAB working directory.

Lab Step 3: Add Callbacks (build and test incrementally)

Rule for this step: Add one callback, run the app, test it, then move to the next. Do not write all callbacks before testing any of them. If an error appears, fix it before moving on — debugging one callback at a time is far easier than debugging five at once.

Step 3.1 — Collect Data Button

In Design View, right-click the Collect Data button and select Callbacks → Add ButtonPushedFcn Callback (ButtonPushedFcn docs). AppDesigner switches to Code View and places the cursor inside the new callback function.

The callback should:

  1. Read the Duration value from the edit field: app.DurationEditField.Value
  2. Read the Delay value from the edit field: app.DelayEditField.Value
  3. Call app.Analyzer.logSensorData(duration, delay)
  4. Read the dropdown value: app.TargetSignalDropDown.Value
  5. Call app.Analyzer.setTargetSignal(dropdownValue)
  6. Plot the signal on the UIAxes:
    matlab plot(app.UIAxes, app.Analyzer.TargetSignalTs, app.Analyzer.TargetSignal)

Note on plotting: The plotTargetSignal method in Lab 4 plots to the current figure. For the GUI, you must plot directly to app.UIAxes rather than calling plotTargetSignal. Use the plot call above, or modify plotTargetSignal to accept a UIAxes handle as an argument.

Run the app. Press Collect Data with a short duration (5 seconds). Wave your phone. The signal should appear in the axes. If the axes remain blank, check that setTargetSignal is being called with a valid signal name.

Step 3.2 — Set Target Signal Dropdown

Add a ValueChangedFcn callback to the dropdown. (ValueChangedFcn docs) When the user changes the dropdown:

  1. Call app.Analyzer.setTargetSignal(app.TargetSignalDropDown.Value)
  2. Re-plot on UIAxes: plot(app.UIAxes, app.Analyzer.TargetSignalTs, app.Analyzer.TargetSignal)

Run and test: collect data, then change the dropdown — the axes should update to the newly selected signal.

Step 3.3 — Smooth Signal Button

Add a ButtonPushedFcn callback to the Smooth Signal button.

The callback should:

  1. Read the Window Size value: app.WindowSizeEditField.Value
  2. Call app.Analyzer.smooth(windowSize)
  3. Re-plot on UIAxes

If smooth is not yet implemented in SensorDataAnalyzer: Add the following method to SensorDataAnalyzer.m and save the file before running the app:
```matlab
function smooth(obj, windowSize)
obj.TargetSignal = movmean(obj.TargetSignal, windowSize);

MathWorks Docs: movmean — moving average using a sliding window; see documentation for endpoint handling options
end
`` SaveSensorDataAnalyzer.m`, then return to the app and test the Smooth Signal button.

Run the app. Collect data. Press Smooth Signal. The signal should visibly smooth — try a Window Size of 10 to confirm the effect is visible.

Step 3.4 — Integrate Target Signal Button

Add a ButtonPushedFcn callback to the Integrate Target Signal button.

The callback should:

  1. Call app.Analyzer.integrate()
  2. Re-plot on UIAxes: plot(app.UIAxes, app.Analyzer.TargetSignalTs, app.Analyzer.TargetSignal)

Run and test. After integrating acceleration, the signal should show velocity (or displacement after a second integration). The shape and units will differ from the raw signal — this is expected.

Step 3.5 — Reset Target Signal Button

The Reset button restores the original unprocessed signal after smoothing or integration. To enable this, SensorDataAnalyzer must store a copy of the original signal.

Modify SensorDataAnalyzer.m:

  1. Add a private property OriginalTargetSignal:
    matlab properties (Access = private) OriginalTargetSignal end

  2. In the setTargetSignal method, store a copy immediately after extracting the signal:
    matlab obj.OriginalTargetSignal = obj.TargetSignal;

  3. Add a reset method:
    matlab function reset(obj) obj.TargetSignal = obj.OriginalTargetSignal; end

Save SensorDataAnalyzer.m. Add a ButtonPushedFcn callback to the Reset Target Signal button:

  1. Call app.Analyzer.reset()
  2. Re-plot on UIAxes

Run and test. Collect data, smooth it, then press Reset — the signal should return to the original unprocessed state, visually matching what was shown immediately after collection.

Canvas Submission Note

Lab 5 does not use MATLAB Grader. The .mlapp file is submitted directly to the Lab 5 assignment on Canvas. Before submitting:
- Run the app and confirm it launches without errors
- Complete the full workflow (collect, smooth, integrate, reset) at least once
- Confirm SensorDataAnalyzer.m is in the same folder as SensorDashboard.mlapp — both files must be accessible for grading

The grader will open your .mlapp file and run the app. If the app fails to launch due to a missing file or unresolved error, the submission cannot be graded.

Lab Step 4: Add a Logo

Create a unique, non-trivial logo image for your sensor dashboard. You can use:
- An AI image generation tool (DALL-E, Midjourney, or similar)
- Photo editing software
- A hand-drawn image, photographed

The logo should be visually distinct and identifiable as yours — not a default placeholder rectangle.

Save the logo as a PNG file in your MiniProject5 folder. In AppDesigner Design View, select the Image component you placed in Step 1 and assign your logo PNG using the Image property field in the Inspector panel on the right.

Lab Step 5: Take Screenshots of the Working App

Run your completed app. Using data from your Lab 4 dead-reckoning experiment — or collect new data now — take the following four screenshots:

Screenshot 1 — Raw Data
Immediately after pressing Collect Data. The axes should show the raw accelerometer signal before any processing.

Screenshot 2 — Smoothed Signal
After pressing Smooth Signal. The signal should be visibly smoother than Screenshot 1.

Screenshot 3 — Integrated Signal
After pressing Integrate Target Signal. The signal now shows the result of numerical integration — it will have a different shape and scale than the raw signal.

Screenshot 4 — Reset Signal
After pressing Reset Target Signal. The signal should return to the original unprocessed state. It should visually match Screenshot 1.

Take these four screenshots before closing the app. Once you close the app, the in-memory state is lost and the screenshots cannot be recreated without repeating the data collection experiment.

Add a descriptive title to each axes state — use the Title field in the AppDesigner Inspector for the UIAxes component, or set it within the callback using title(app.UIAxes, 'Raw Signal') etc. — so each screenshot is clearly identifiable.

Lab Session Checklist

Before leaving, confirm you have:

  • [ ] SensorDashboard.mlapp saved and running without errors
  • [ ] All four callbacks implemented and tested (Collect Data, Smooth Signal, Integrate, Reset)
  • [ ] Dropdown callback implemented and working
  • [ ] Logo PNG saved in MiniProject5 folder and assigned in AppDesigner
  • [ ] Screenshot 1 — Raw data — taken and saved
  • [ ] Screenshot 2 — Smoothed signal — taken and saved
  • [ ] Screenshot 3 — Integrated signal — taken and saved
  • [ ] Screenshot 4 — Reset signal — taken and saved
  • [ ] SensorDataAnalyzer.m in the same folder as the .mlapp file
  • [ ] .mlapp file ready for Canvas submission

POST-LAB

Complete within 48 hours of your lab session.

You should now have: four app screenshots saved, your Pre-Lab AI summaries written, and your Self-Test record from Part 2. The post-lab submission collects this work into a structured package.

Submission Instructions

Your work is submitted in two places.

Part A: Canvas

Submit SensorDashboard.mlapp to the Lab 5 assignment on Canvas. The .mlapp file is your code submission for this lab — there is no separate MATLAB Grader step.

Part B: JSON App Submission

Open the course submission app, load the Lab 5 schema file, and complete all fields. Upload the generated JSON file to Gradescope under the Lab 5 assignment.

Field 1 — Pre-Lab AI Exploration Summary (reflective)

In 3–5 sentences, summarize in your own words what you learned from Pre-Lab Part 1. Your summary must address:
- What goes wrong in practice when GUI code and analysis code are mixed together?
- What specific capability does keeping them separate preserve?

Write this from your notes. Do not paste AI output directly.

Field 2 — Self-Test Record (reflective)

Describe the scenario the AI gave you during Pre-Lab Part 2. In your own words — not a paste — record:
- The scenario: what was the application, what went wrong when GUI and analysis were mixed
- Your classification: which type of problem did it illustrate — testability, reusability, or fragility (more than one may apply)
- Your reasoning for the classification

Field 3 — Screenshot: Raw Data (image upload)

Upload a screenshot of your running app showing the raw accelerometer signal immediately after collection. The signal should be visible in the UIAxes. No processing should have been applied yet.

Requirements:
- The full app window is visible, including buttons and controls
- The raw signal is displayed in the axes
- Screenshot taken while the app is running (not a saved PNG from a separate script)

Field 4 — Screenshot: Smoothed Signal (image upload)

Upload a screenshot of your running app after pressing Smooth Signal. The signal in the axes should be visibly smoother than the raw signal in Field 3.

Requirements:
- The full app window is visible
- The smoothed signal is displayed in the axes
- Visibly different from Field 3 (smoothing effect is apparent)

Field 5 — Screenshot: Integrated Signal (image upload)

Upload a screenshot of your running app after pressing Integrate Target Signal. The signal in the axes should reflect the result of numerical integration — a different shape and scale from the raw signal.

Requirements:
- The full app window is visible
- An integrated signal is displayed in the axes
- Signal shape visibly differs from Field 3

Field 6 — Screenshot: Reset Signal (image upload)

Upload a screenshot of your running app after pressing Reset Target Signal. The signal should have returned to the original unprocessed state — it should visually match Field 3.

Requirements:
- The full app window is visible
- The signal matches the raw signal from Field 3
- Demonstrates that reset restored the original signal after processing

Field 7 — Formal Reflection (reflective)

Write a reflection of 150–200 words as continuous prose — not a bulleted list, not three separate paragraphs, but a single coherent argument.

Your reflection must accomplish three things in order:

The Link: Connect the two-lab structure to the principle that interface and logic serve different purposes and change for different reasons. The GUI and the analysis class are separate because they change independently — the GUI changes when users need different controls; the analysis changes when the algorithm improves. Articulate why mixing them means either change risks breaking the other.

The Technical Why: Name at least one concrete consequence of mixing GUI and analysis code — something that becomes impossible or significantly harder. Examples: you cannot test the analysis without launching the full GUI; you cannot reuse the analysis in a different context (a script, a web interface); a change to the layout risks breaking working analysis code.

The Lab Application: Describe a specific moment in Labs 4 or 5 where the separation was practically useful to you. Reference your own experience — a method you tested from the command line before the GUI existed, or a callback you modified without touching the analysis. Be specific about what you did and what the separation made possible.

Grading note: "It's cleaner to separate them" or "separation of concerns is a principle" will not satisfy The Technical Why — name a specific practical consequence. A generic software design statement without a personal moment from the two labs will not satisfy The Lab Application.

Include your word count at the end.

Post-Lab Checklist

Before submitting, confirm:

  • [ ] SensorDashboard.mlapp submitted to Canvas
  • [ ] Field 1: AI Exploration summary in your own words — addresses what goes wrong when mixed, and what separation preserves
  • [ ] Field 2: Self-Test record — scenario, classification (testability / reusability / fragility), reasoning
  • [ ] Field 3: Raw data screenshot — full app visible, raw signal in axes
  • [ ] Field 4: Smoothed signal screenshot — visibly smoother than Field 3
  • [ ] Field 5: Integrated signal screenshot — visibly different shape from raw
  • [ ] Field 6: Reset signal screenshot — signal matches Field 3
  • [ ] Field 7: Formal reflection, 150–200 words, continuous prose, word count included
  • [ ] JSON file generated and uploaded to Gradescope

Rubric

Rubric
Component What is assessed Where graded
SensorDashboard.mlapp App launches without errors; all five callbacks implemented and connected; logo present; axes display signal correctly Human review (Canvas)
Field 1: AI Exploration Summary Demonstrates understanding of what mixing GUI and analysis code costs in practice; separation is explained in terms of a specific capability, not just "cleaner code" Custom AI grader
Field 2: Self-Test Record Scenario described in own words; classification is correct and reasoned, not just named; shows applied understanding rather than rote recall Custom AI grader
Field 3: Raw data screenshot App window visible; raw signal in axes; no visible processing applied Human review
Field 4: Smoothed signal screenshot Signal visibly smoother than Field 3; app fully visible Human review
Field 5: Integrated signal screenshot Signal shape visibly differs from raw; app fully visible Human review
Field 6: Reset signal screenshot Signal matches Field 3; demonstrates reset functionality Human review
Field 7: Formal Reflection The Link, The Technical Why, and The Lab Application all addressed substantively; The Lab Application references a specific personal moment from Labs 4 or 5; continuous prose; word count included Custom AI grader

Academic integrity: Your screenshots must be taken from an app you built and ran yourself. Submitted screenshots that are inconsistent with your .mlapp code, or that show a different student's name or interface, will be investigated. The Canvas submission will be opened and run during grading.
